name: Andromeda
mythology: Andromeda was the daughter of King Cepheus and Queen Cassiopeia of Ethiopia. Her mother boasted that Andromeda was more beautiful than the Nereids, angering Poseidon who sent a sea monster to ravage the coast. To appease the monster, Andromeda was chained to a rock as a sacrifice. Perseus, returning from slaying Medusa, saw her, fell in love, and used Medusa's head to turn the monster to stone. They married and she was placed among the stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Andromeda was the daughter of King Cepheus and Queen Cassiopeia of Ethiopia — You found Andromeda.
mastery_badge_caption: Andromeda was the daughter of King Cepheus and Queen Cassiopeia of Ethiopia — You traced Andromeda among the stars.

name: Antlia
mythology: Antlia represents an air pump. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the invention of the air pump by Otto von Guericke. It lies in a relatively empty region of the southern sky and contains few bright stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Antlia represents an air pump — You found Antlia.
mastery_badge_caption: Antlia represents an air pump — You traced Antlia among the stars.

name: Apus
mythology: Apus represents a bird-of-paradise. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Pieter Dirkszoon Keyser and Frederick de Houtman in the late 16th century, and first depicted on a globe by Petrus Plancius in 1598. Its name derives from the Greek word apous meaning footless, as it was once believed the bird-of-paradise had no feet.
discovery_badge_caption: Apus represents a bird-of-paradise — You found Apus.
mastery_badge_caption: Apus represents a bird-of-paradise — You traced Apus among the stars.

name: Aquarius
mythology: Aquarius represents a water-bearer, a figure pouring water from a jar. In Greek mythology, it is associated with Ganymede, the beautiful youth whom Zeus carried off to Mount Olympus to serve as cupbearer to the gods. The stream of water is depicted flowing into the mouth of the Southern Fish, represented by the neighboring constellation Piscis Austrinus.
discovery_badge_caption: Aquarius represents a water-bearer, a figure pouring water from a jar — You found Aquarius.
mastery_badge_caption: Aquarius represents a water-bearer, a figure pouring water from a jar — You traced Aquarius among the stars.

name: Aquila
mythology: Aquila represents an eagle. In Greek mythology, this eagle carried Zeus's thunderbolts and was sent to abduct Ganymede. The eagle is also associated with the story of Prometheus, whose liver was eaten daily by an eagle as punishment for giving fire to humanity. Its brightest star, Altair, forms one vertex of the Summer Triangle.
discovery_badge_caption: Aquila represents an eagle — You found Aquila.
mastery_badge_caption: Aquila represents an eagle — You traced Aquila among the stars.

name: Ara
mythology: Ara represents an altar. In Greek mythology, it is identified as the altar where the Olympian gods swore allegiance before their battle against the Titans. It was placed in the sky by Zeus as a reminder of the victory. The constellation lies near the center of the Milky Way and contains many bright star clusters.
discovery_badge_caption: Ara represents an altar — You found Ara.
mastery_badge_caption: Ara represents an altar — You traced Ara among the stars.

name: Aries
mythology: Aries represents a ram. In Greek mythology, it represents the winged ram Chrysomallus that carried Phrixus and Helle to safety. Phrixus sacrificed the ram and hung its Golden Fleece in a sacred grove, which later became the quest of Jason and the Argonauts. Its brightest star is Hamal.
discovery_badge_caption: Aries represents a ram — You found Aries.
mastery_badge_caption: Aries represents a ram — You traced Aries among the stars.

name: Auriga
mythology: Auriga represents a charioteer. It is associated with several figures from Greek mythology, most commonly Erichthonius, the son of Hephaestus who invented the four-horse chariot. The constellation contains Capella, the sixth-brightest star in the night sky, and three prominent open clusters known as the Kids.
discovery_badge_caption: Auriga represents a charioteer — You found Auriga.
mastery_badge_caption: Auriga represents a charioteer — You traced Auriga among the stars.

name: Boötes
mythology: Boötes represents a herdsman or ploughman. In Greek mythology, he is often identified as Arcas, son of Callisto, who was transformed into a bear and placed in the sky as Ursa Major. Boötes drives the bears around the celestial pole with his hunting dogs. Its brightest star, Arcturus, is one of the brightest in the northern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Boötes represents a herdsman or ploughman — You found Boötes.
mastery_badge_caption: Boötes represents a herdsman or ploughman — You traced Boötes among the stars.

name: Caelum
mythology: Caelum represents a chisel or engraving tool. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to fill the southern skies with instruments of the arts and sciences. It is a faint constellation with no stars brighter than fourth magnitude, lying between Columba and Eridanus.
discovery_badge_caption: Caelum represents a chisel or engraving tool — You found Caelum.
mastery_badge_caption: Caelum represents a chisel or engraving tool — You traced Caelum among the stars.

name: Camelopardalis
mythology: Camelopardalis represents a giraffe. It was introduced by Petrus Plancius in the early 17th century. The name derives from the Latin for camel and leopard, as Europeans believed the giraffe was a cross between these two animals. It is a large but faint constellation near the north celestial pole.
discovery_badge_caption: Camelopardalis represents a giraffe — You found Camelopardalis.
mastery_badge_caption: Camelopardalis represents a giraffe — You traced Camelopardalis among the stars.

name: Cancer
mythology: Cancer represents a crab. In Greek mythology, it is the crab that Hera sent to distract Heracles during his battle with the Hydra. The crab was crushed by Heracles, but Hera placed it in the sky as a reward for its efforts. It contains the Beehive Cluster, a bright open cluster visible to the naked eye.
discovery_badge_caption: Cancer represents a crab — You found Cancer.
mastery_badge_caption: Cancer represents a crab — You traced Cancer among the stars.

name: Canes Venatici
mythology: Canes Venatici represents the hunting dogs of Boötes. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century. The two dogs are named Asterion and Chara, and the constellation contains the bright binary star Cor Caroli, named by Edmond Halley in honor of King Charles II.
discovery_badge_caption: Canes Venatici represents the hunting dogs of Boötes — You found Canes Venatici.
mastery_badge_caption: Canes Venatici represents the hunting dogs of Boötes — You traced Canes Venatici among the stars.

name: Canis Major
mythology: Canis Major represents the greater dog, one of Orion's hunting dogs. In Greek mythology, it is Laelaps, the swiftest hound, who was placed in the sky after chasing the Teumessian fox. It contains Sirius, the Dog Star, the brightest star in the night sky, whose rising in July marked the hottest days of summer for the ancient Greeks.
discovery_badge_caption: Canis Major represents the greater dog, one of Orion's hunting dogs — You found Canis Major.
mastery_badge_caption: Canis Major represents the greater dog, one of Orion's hunting dogs — You traced Canis Major among the stars.

name: Canis Minor
mythology: Canis Minor represents the lesser dog, Orion's smaller hunting dog. In Greek mythology, it is associated with Maera, the dog of Icarius, who led his daughter Erigone to his grave after he was killed by shepherds. Its brightest star, Procyon, rises just before Sirius and was used as a herald of the Dog Star.
discovery_badge_caption: Canis Minor represents the lesser dog, Orion's smaller hunting dog — You found Canis Minor.
mastery_badge_caption: Canis Minor represents the lesser dog, Orion's smaller hunting dog — You traced Canis Minor among the stars.

name: Capricornus
mythology: Capricornus represents a sea-goat, a creature with the front of a goat and the tail of a fish. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the goat Amalthea who nursed the infant Zeus, and also with the god Pan who transformed into a fish-goat hybrid to escape the monster Typhon. It is the smallest zodiac constellation.
discovery_badge_caption: Capricornus represents a sea-goat, a creature with the front of a goat and the tail of a fish — You found Capricornus.
mastery_badge_caption: Capricornus represents a sea-goat, a creature with the front of a goat and the tail of a fish — You traced Capricornus among the stars.

name: Carina
mythology: Carina represents the keel of a ship. It was originally part of the larger constellation Argo Navis, representing the ship of Jason and the Argonauts. Carina was split from Argo Navis by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century. It contains Canopus, the second-brightest star in the night sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Carina represents the keel of a ship — You found Carina.
mastery_badge_caption: Carina represents the keel of a ship — You traced Carina among the stars.

name: Cassiopeia
mythology: Cassiopeia represents Queen Cassiopeia, the vain wife of King Cepheus of Ethiopia. She boasted that she and her daughter Andromeda were more beautiful than the Nereids, angering Poseidon. As punishment, she was placed in the sky chained to her throne, where she circles the celestial pole upside down half the year.
discovery_badge_caption: Cassiopeia represents Queen Cassiopeia, the vain wife of King Cepheus of Ethiopia — You found Cassiopeia.
mastery_badge_caption: Cassiopeia represents Queen Cassiopeia, the vain wife of King Cepheus of Ethiopia — You traced Cassiopeia among the stars.

name: Centaurus
mythology: Centaurus represents a centaur, a mythical creature half-human and half-horse. It is usually identified with Chiron, the wise centaur who tutored many Greek heroes including Achilles and Heracles. It contains Alpha Centauri, the closest star system to Earth, and Omega Centauri, the largest globular cluster in the sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Centaurus represents a centaur, a mythical creature half-human and half-horse — You found Centaurus.
mastery_badge_caption: Centaurus represents a centaur, a mythical creature half-human and half-horse — You traced Centaurus among the stars.

name: Cepheus
mythology: Cepheus represents King Cepheus, husband of Cassiopeia and father of Andromeda in Greek mythology. He was the king of Ethiopia and was placed in the sky alongside his family. The constellation contains the prototype Cepheid variable star Delta Cephei, which is crucial for measuring cosmic distances.
discovery_badge_caption: Cepheus represents King Cepheus, husband of Cassiopeia and father of Andromeda in Greek mythology — You found Cepheus.
mastery_badge_caption: Cepheus represents King Cepheus, husband of Cassiopeia and father of Andromeda in Greek mythology — You traced Cepheus among the stars.

name: Cetus
mythology: Cetus represents a sea monster. In Greek mythology, it was the monster sent by Poseidon to ravage Ethiopia after Cassiopeia's boast. Perseus turned it to stone using Medusa's head while rescuing Andromeda. Despite its name meaning whale, it is typically depicted as a serpent-like sea creature.
discovery_badge_caption: Cetus represents a sea monster — You found Cetus.
mastery_badge_caption: Cetus represents a sea monster — You traced Cetus among the stars.

name: Chamaeleon
mythology: Chamaeleon represents a chameleon. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It is a faint southern constellation with no stars brighter than fourth magnitude, lying near the south celestial pole.
discovery_badge_caption: Chamaeleon represents a chameleon — You found Chamaeleon.
mastery_badge_caption: Chamaeleon represents a chameleon — You traced Chamaeleon among the stars.

name: Circinus
mythology: Circinus represents a pair of compasses, the drafting tool. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the instruments of science and art. It is a small, faint constellation in the southern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Circinus represents a pair of compasses, the drafting tool — You found Circinus.
mastery_badge_caption: Circinus represents a pair of compasses, the drafting tool — You traced Circinus among the stars.

name: Columba
mythology: Columba represents a dove. It was introduced by Petrus Plancius in the early 17th century to represent the dove that Noah sent from the Ark. It lies south of Canis Major and Lepus, and contains the bright star Phact.
discovery_badge_caption: Columba represents a dove — You found Columba.
mastery_badge_caption: Columba represents a dove — You traced Columba among the stars.

name: Coma Berenices
mythology: Coma Berenices represents the hair of Queen Berenice II of Egypt. According to legend, she cut off her hair and offered it to the gods for her husband's safe return from war. The hair was placed in the sky by the court astronomer. It is the only constellation named after a historical figure.
discovery_badge_caption: Coma Berenices represents the hair of Queen Berenice II of Egypt — You found Coma Berenices.
mastery_badge_caption: Coma Berenices represents the hair of Queen Berenice II of Egypt — You traced Coma Berenices among the stars.

name: Corona Australis
mythology: Corona Australis represents the southern crown. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the crown of the centaur Sagittarius, or alternatively the wreath placed in the sky by Dionysus. It is a small crescent-shaped constellation near the Milky Way.
discovery_badge_caption: Corona Australis represents the southern crown — You found Corona Australis.
mastery_badge_caption: Corona Australis represents the southern crown — You traced Corona Australis among the stars.

name: Corona Borealis
mythology: Corona Borealis represents the northern crown. In Greek mythology, it is the crown of Ariadne, given to her by Dionysus on their wedding day. When she died, Dionysus threw the crown into the sky where the jewels became stars. Its brightest star is Alphecca.
discovery_badge_caption: Corona Borealis represents the northern crown — You found Corona Borealis.
mastery_badge_caption: Corona Borealis represents the northern crown — You traced Corona Borealis among the stars.

name: Corvus
mythology: Corvus represents a crow or raven. In Greek mythology, the crow was sent by Apollo to fetch water, but it delayed and brought back a water snake instead, blaming the snake for the delay. Apollo saw through the lie and placed all three in the sky. The crow is depicted perched on Hydra's tail.
discovery_badge_caption: Corvus represents a crow or raven — You found Corvus.
mastery_badge_caption: Corvus represents a crow or raven — You traced Corvus among the stars.

name: Crater
mythology: Crater represents a krater, a large wine-mixing bowl used in ancient Greece. In mythology, it is associated with the crow Corvus and the water snake Hydra. Apollo sent the crow to fetch water with the bowl, but the crow tarried and brought back a water snake, leading to all three being placed in the sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Crater represents a krater, a large wine-mixing bowl used in ancient Greece — You found Crater.
mastery_badge_caption: Crater represents a krater, a large wine-mixing bowl used in ancient Greece — You traced Crater among the stars.

name: Crux
mythology: Crux represents a Christian cross. It was separated from Centaurus by Petrus Plancius in the late 16th century. Despite being the smallest constellation by area, it contains several bright stars including Acrux and Becrux, and is a prominent feature of the southern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Crux represents a Christian cross — You found Crux.
mastery_badge_caption: Crux represents a Christian cross — You traced Crux among the stars.

name: Cygnus
mythology: Cygnus represents a swan. In Greek mythology, it is associated with several stories: Zeus transformed into a swan to seduce Leda; Orpheus was placed in the sky as a swan after his death; and Cygnus was a friend of Phaëthon who was turned into a swan. Its brightest star, Deneb, forms the top of the Northern Cross asterism.
discovery_badge_caption: Cygnus represents a swan — You found Cygnus.
mastery_badge_caption: Cygnus represents a swan — You traced Cygnus among the stars.

name: Delphinus
mythology: Delphinus represents a dolphin. In Greek mythology, it represents the dolphin that persuaded the sea nymph Amphitrite to marry Poseidon. As a reward, Poseidon placed the dolphin in the sky. In another story, it saved the poet Arion from drowning.
discovery_badge_caption: Delphinus represents a dolphin — You found Delphinus.
mastery_badge_caption: Delphinus represents a dolphin — You traced Delphinus among the stars.

name: Dorado
mythology: Dorado represents a dolphinfish, also known as mahi-mahi. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It contains most of the Large Magellanic Cloud, a satellite galaxy of the Milky Way.
discovery_badge_caption: Dorado represents a dolphinfish, also known as mahi-mahi — You found Dorado.
mastery_badge_caption: Dorado represents a dolphinfish, also known as mahi-mahi — You traced Dorado among the stars.

name: Draco
mythology: Draco represents a dragon. In Greek mythology, it is Ladon, the hundred-headed dragon that guarded the golden apples of the Hesperides. Heracles slew it as one of his labors, and Hera placed it in the sky. It winds around the north celestial pole and its brightest star is Eltanin.
discovery_badge_caption: Draco represents a dragon — You found Draco.
mastery_badge_caption: Draco represents a dragon — You traced Draco among the stars.

name: Equuleus
mythology: Equuleus represents a small horse or pony. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the foal Celeris, the brother of Pegasus, or with the horse struck from Poseidon's trident. It is the second-smallest constellation and lies near the head of Pegasus.
discovery_badge_caption: Equuleus represents a small horse or pony — You found Equuleus.
mastery_badge_caption: Equuleus represents a small horse or pony — You traced Equuleus among the stars.

name: Eridanus
mythology: Eridanus represents a river. In Greek mythology, it is the river Po in Italy, associated with the story of Phaëthon, who lost control of the sun chariot and fell into the river. It is a long, winding constellation stretching from near Orion to the southern sky, with Achernar at its southern end.
discovery_badge_caption: Eridanus represents a river — You found Eridanus.
mastery_badge_caption: Eridanus represents a river — You traced Eridanus among the stars.

name: Fornax
mythology: Fornax represents a chemical furnace. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century as Fornax Chemiae to commemorate the chemical furnace used in experiments. It is a faint constellation in the southern sky with no bright stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Fornax represents a chemical furnace — You found Fornax.
mastery_badge_caption: Fornax represents a chemical furnace — You traced Fornax among the stars.

name: Gemini
mythology: Gemini represents the twin brothers Castor and Pollux. In Greek mythology, Castor was mortal and Pollux was divine, sons of Leda. When Castor died, Pollux asked Zeus to share his immortality, so they alternate between Olympus and the underworld. Their constellation is a zodiac sign.
discovery_badge_caption: Gemini represents the twin brothers Castor and Pollux — You found Gemini.
mastery_badge_caption: Gemini represents the twin brothers Castor and Pollux — You traced Gemini among the stars.

name: Grus
mythology: Grus represents a crane, the bird. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It lies in the southern sky and contains the bright star Alnair. Its neighbors include Piscis Austrinus and Tucana.
discovery_badge_caption: Grus represents a crane, the bird — You found Grus.
mastery_badge_caption: Grus represents a crane, the bird — You traced Grus among the stars.

name: Hercules
mythology: Hercules represents the legendary Greek hero Heracles. In Greek mythology, he was the son of Zeus and Alcmene, known for his twelve labors. The constellation depicts him kneeling, holding a club. It contains the Great Globular Cluster in Hercules, one of the finest in the northern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Hercules represents the legendary Greek hero Heracles — You found Hercules.
mastery_badge_caption: Hercules represents the legendary Greek hero Heracles — You traced Hercules among the stars.

name: Horologium
mythology: Horologium represents a pendulum clock. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the invention of the pendulum clock by Christiaan Huygens. It is a faint constellation in the southern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Horologium represents a pendulum clock — You found Horologium.
mastery_badge_caption: Horologium represents a pendulum clock — You traced Horologium among the stars.

name: Hydra
mythology: Hydra represents the water snake, the largest constellation by area. In Greek mythology, it is the Lernaean Hydra, a nine-headed serpent slain by Heracles as his second labor. Each time one head was cut off, two grew back. Heracles was assisted by his nephew Iolaus, who cauterized the necks.
discovery_badge_caption: Hydra represents the water snake, the largest constellation by area — You found Hydra.
mastery_badge_caption: Hydra represents the water snake, the largest constellation by area — You traced Hydra among the stars.

name: Hydrus
mythology: Hydrus represents a lesser water snake. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It lies in the southern sky near the south celestial pole and should not be confused with the larger Hydra.
discovery_badge_caption: Hydrus represents a lesser water snake — You found Hydrus.
mastery_badge_caption: Hydrus represents a lesser water snake — You traced Hydrus among the stars.

name: Indus
mythology: Indus represents an Indian, referring to the indigenous peoples of the East Indies. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It lies in the southern sky and contains no bright stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Indus represents an Indian, referring to the indigenous peoples of the East Indies — You found Indus.
mastery_badge_caption: Indus represents an Indian, referring to the indigenous peoples of the East Indies — You traced Indus among the stars.

name: Lacerta
mythology: Lacerta represents a lizard. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century to fill a gap between Andromeda and Cygnus. It is a faint constellation with no stars brighter than fourth magnitude.
discovery_badge_caption: Lacerta represents a lizard — You found Lacerta.
mastery_badge_caption: Lacerta represents a lizard — You traced Lacerta among the stars.

name: Leo
mythology: Leo represents a lion. In Greek mythology, it is the Nemean lion slain by Heracles as his first labor. The lion was invulnerable to weapons, so Heracles strangled it with his bare hands. Zeus placed it in the sky as a monument to Heracles' strength. Its brightest star is Regulus.
discovery_badge_caption: Leo represents a lion — You found Leo.
mastery_badge_caption: Leo represents a lion — You traced Leo among the stars.

name: Leo Minor
mythology: Leo Minor represents a lesser lion. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century to fill the area between Leo and Ursa Major. It is a faint constellation with no stars brighter than fourth magnitude.
discovery_badge_caption: Leo Minor represents a lesser lion — You found Leo Minor.
mastery_badge_caption: Leo Minor represents a lesser lion — You traced Leo Minor among the stars.

name: Lepus
mythology: Lepus represents a hare. In Greek mythology, it is the hare being hunted by Orion and his dogs. It lies directly south of Orion and contains several bright stars including Arneb and Nihal. The constellation contains the globular cluster M79.
discovery_badge_caption: Lepus represents a hare — You found Lepus.
mastery_badge_caption: Lepus represents a hare — You traced Lepus among the stars.

name: Libra
mythology: Libra represents a balance or weighing scale. In Roman mythology, it represents the scales of justice held by Astraea, the goddess of justice. It was once considered the claws of Scorpius, but became a separate constellation. It is the only zodiac symbol not represented by a living creature.
discovery_badge_caption: Libra represents a balance or weighing scale — You found Libra.
mastery_badge_caption: Libra represents a balance or weighing scale — You traced Libra among the stars.

name: Lupus
mythology: Lupus represents a wolf. It was considered by the ancient Greeks as a wild animal being sacrificed by the centaur Centaurus. It lies near the Milky Way in the southern sky and contains many bright stars including Alpha Lupi.
discovery_badge_caption: Lupus represents a wolf — You found Lupus.
mastery_badge_caption: Lupus represents a wolf — You traced Lupus among the stars.

name: Lynx
mythology: Lynx represents a lynx. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century, who named it after the sharp-eyed cat because only the lynx-eyed could see its faint stars. It is a large but dim constellation between Ursa Major and Auriga.
discovery_badge_caption: Lynx represents a lynx — You found Lynx.
mastery_badge_caption: Lynx represents a lynx — You traced Lynx among the stars.

name: Lyra
mythology: Lyra represents a lyre, the musical instrument. In Greek mythology, it is the lyre of Orpheus, the legendary musician whose music could charm animals and even stones. After his death, Zeus placed his lyre in the sky. Its brightest star, Vega, is one of the brightest in the northern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Lyra represents a lyre, the musical instrument — You found Lyra.
mastery_badge_caption: Lyra represents a lyre, the musical instrument — You traced Lyra among the stars.

name: Mensa
mythology: Mensa represents Table Mountain in South Africa. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century, who named it Mons Mensae. It lies near the south celestial pole and contains part of the Large Magellanic Cloud.
discovery_badge_caption: Mensa represents Table Mountain in South Africa — You found Mensa.
mastery_badge_caption: Mensa represents Table Mountain in South Africa — You traced Mensa among the stars.

name: Microscopium
mythology: Microscopium represents a microscope. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the scientific instrument. It is a faint constellation in the southern sky with no bright stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Microscopium represents a microscope — You found Microscopium.
mastery_badge_caption: Microscopium represents a microscope — You traced Microscopium among the stars.

name: Monoceros
mythology: Monoceros represents a unicorn. It was introduced by Petrus Plancius in the early 17th century. Its name means single horn in Greek. It lies in the middle of the Winter Triangle and contains several beautiful star clusters and nebulae.
discovery_badge_caption: Monoceros represents a unicorn — You found Monoceros.
mastery_badge_caption: Monoceros represents a unicorn — You traced Monoceros among the stars.

name: Musca
mythology: Musca represents a fly. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It is one of the few constellations named after an insect and lies near the south celestial pole. It contains the bright globular cluster NGC 4833.
discovery_badge_caption: Musca represents a fly — You found Musca.
mastery_badge_caption: Musca represents a fly — You traced Musca among the stars.

name: Norma
mythology: Norma represents a carpenter's square, the measuring tool. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the instruments of science. It is a faint constellation lying near the Milky Way in the southern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Norma represents a carpenter's square, the measuring tool — You found Norma.
mastery_badge_caption: Norma represents a carpenter's square, the measuring tool — You traced Norma among the stars.

name: Octans
mythology: Octans represents an octant, the navigational instrument. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century. It contains the south celestial pole, marked by the faint star Sigma Octantis, the southern counterpart to Polaris.
discovery_badge_caption: Octans represents an octant, the navigational instrument — You found Octans.
mastery_badge_caption: Octans represents an octant, the navigational instrument — You traced Octans among the stars.

name: Ophiuchus
mythology: Ophiuchus represents a serpent-bearer. In Greek mythology, it is identified with Asclepius, the god of medicine, who learned the secrets of life and death by observing a snake. Zeus killed him for resurrecting the dead but later placed him in the sky. He holds the serpent Serpens.
discovery_badge_caption: Ophiuchus represents a serpent-bearer — You found Ophiuchus.
mastery_badge_caption: Ophiuchus represents a serpent-bearer — You traced Ophiuchus among the stars.

name: Orion
mythology: Orion represents the great hunter of Greek mythology. Orion boasted he could kill any animal on Earth, angering Gaia who sent a giant scorpion to kill him. After his death, Zeus placed him in the sky with his two dogs, Canis Major and Canis Minor. He pursues the Pleiades and flees from Scorpius.
discovery_badge_caption: Orion represents the great hunter of Greek mythology — You found Orion.
mastery_badge_caption: Orion represents the great hunter of Greek mythology — You traced Orion among the stars.

name: Pavo
mythology: Pavo represents a peacock. In Greek mythology, it was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It was associated with the peacock, the sacred bird of Hera, whose tail bears the eyes of the hundred-eyed giant Argus.
discovery_badge_caption: Pavo represents a peacock — You found Pavo.
mastery_badge_caption: Pavo represents a peacock — You traced Pavo among the stars.

name: Pegasus
mythology: Pegasus represents the winged horse of Greek mythology. Pegasus sprang from the blood of Medusa after Perseus beheaded her. He was tamed by the hero Bellerophon, who rode him to slay the Chimera. Zeus placed him in the sky as a constellation. Its square pattern is a famous asterism.
discovery_badge_caption: Pegasus represents the winged horse of Greek mythology — You found Pegasus.
mastery_badge_caption: Pegasus represents the winged horse of Greek mythology — You traced Pegasus among the stars.

name: Perseus
mythology: Perseus represents the Greek hero who slew Medusa and rescued Andromeda. He was the son of Zeus and Danaë, and used a mirrored shield to avoid Medusa's petrifying gaze. After his adventures, he married Andromeda and was placed in the sky alongside her, her parents, and the sea monster.
discovery_badge_caption: Perseus represents the Greek hero who slew Medusa and rescued Andromeda — You found Perseus.
mastery_badge_caption: Perseus represents the Greek hero who slew Medusa and rescued Andromeda — You traced Perseus among the stars.

name: Phoenix
mythology: Phoenix represents a phoenix, the mythical bird that rises from its own ashes. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. In Egyptian and Greek mythology, the phoenix cyclically regenerates, symbolizing renewal and immortality.
discovery_badge_caption: Phoenix represents a phoenix, the mythical bird that rises from its own ashes — You found Phoenix.
mastery_badge_caption: Phoenix represents a phoenix, the mythical bird that rises from its own ashes — You traced Phoenix among the stars.

name: Pictor
mythology: Pictor represents a painter's easel. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century as Equuleus Pictoris. It lies south of Columba and west of Carina, and contains the star Beta Pictoris, famous for its circumstellar disk.
discovery_badge_caption: Pictor represents a painter's easel — You found Pictor.
mastery_badge_caption: Pictor represents a painter's easel — You traced Pictor among the stars.

name: Pisces
mythology: Pisces represents a pair of fish. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the story of Aphrodite and Eros, who transformed into fish and tied themselves together with a cord to escape the monster Typhon. It is a zodiac constellation and contains the vernal equinox.
discovery_badge_caption: Pisces represents a pair of fish — You found Pisces.
mastery_badge_caption: Pisces represents a pair of fish — You traced Pisces among the stars.

name: Piscis Austrinus
mythology: Piscis Austrinus represents the southern fish. In Greek mythology, it is the parent of the two fish of Pisces, or the fish that saved the Syrian goddess Derketo. It contains Fomalhaut, one of the brightest stars in the southern sky, which marks the mouth of the fish.
discovery_badge_caption: Piscis Austrinus represents the southern fish — You found Piscis Austrinus.
mastery_badge_caption: Piscis Austrinus represents the southern fish — You traced Piscis Austrinus among the stars.

name: Puppis
mythology: Puppis represents the poop deck or stern of a ship. It was originally part of Argo Navis, the ship of Jason and the Argonauts, and was split off by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille. It lies in the Milky Way and contains many bright open clusters.
discovery_badge_caption: Puppis represents the poop deck or stern of a ship — You found Puppis.
mastery_badge_caption: Puppis represents the poop deck or stern of a ship — You traced Puppis among the stars.

name: Pyxis
mythology: Pyxis represents a mariner's compass. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century as Pyxis Nautica. It lies near the original Argo Navis constellation and contains few bright stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Pyxis represents a mariner's compass — You found Pyxis.
mastery_badge_caption: Pyxis represents a mariner's compass — You traced Pyxis among the stars.

name: Reticulum
mythology: Reticulum represents a reticle, the crosshairs in a telescope eyepiece. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the measuring instrument. It is a small, faint constellation in the southern sky.
discovery_badge_caption: Reticulum represents a reticle, the crosshairs in a telescope eyepiece — You found Reticulum.
mastery_badge_caption: Reticulum represents a reticle, the crosshairs in a telescope eyepiece — You traced Reticulum among the stars.

name: Sagitta
mythology: Sagitta represents an arrow. In Greek mythology, it is the arrow shot by Heracles toward the Stymphalian birds, or the arrow of Eros. Despite being the third-smallest constellation, it has been recognized since ancient times and was listed by Ptolemy.
discovery_badge_caption: Sagitta represents an arrow — You found Sagitta.
mastery_badge_caption: Sagitta represents an arrow — You traced Sagitta among the stars.

name: Sagittarius
mythology: Sagittarius represents an archer. In Greek mythology, it is usually identified with the centaur Chiron, a wise teacher of heroes. He is depicted drawing his bow, aiming at the heart of Scorpius. The center of the Milky Way lies in Sagittarius, making it rich in star clusters and nebulae.
discovery_badge_caption: Sagittarius represents an archer — You found Sagittarius.
mastery_badge_caption: Sagittarius represents an archer — You traced Sagittarius among the stars.

name: Scorpius
mythology: Scorpius represents a scorpion. In Greek mythology, it is the scorpion sent by Gaia to kill the hunter Orion after he boasted he would kill all animals. The scorpion's sting proved fatal, and both were placed in the sky on opposite sides, so they never rise together. Its brightest star is Antares.
discovery_badge_caption: Scorpius represents a scorpion — You found Scorpius.
mastery_badge_caption: Scorpius represents a scorpion — You traced Scorpius among the stars.

name: Sculptor
mythology: Sculptor represents a sculptor's studio. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century as Apparatus Sculptoris. It is a faint constellation in the southern sky that contains the Sculptor Group of galaxies, the nearest group to the Local Group.
discovery_badge_caption: Sculptor represents a sculptor's studio — You found Sculptor.
mastery_badge_caption: Sculptor represents a sculptor's studio — You traced Sculptor among the stars.

name: Scutum
mythology: Scutum represents a shield. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century as Scutum Sobiescianum, honoring King John III Sobieski of Poland. It is a small constellation in the Milky Way, containing the Wild Duck Cluster and the Scutum Star Cloud.
discovery_badge_caption: Scutum represents a shield — You found Scutum.
mastery_badge_caption: Scutum represents a shield — You traced Scutum among the stars.

name: Serpens
mythology: Serpens represents a snake held by the serpent-bearer Ophiuchus. It is divided into two parts: Serpens Caputa and Serpens Cauda. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the snake that gave Asclepius the knowledge of medicine. It is the only constellation divided into two separate regions.
discovery_badge_caption: Serpens represents a snake held by the serpent-bearer Ophiuchus — You found Serpens.
mastery_badge_caption: Serpens represents a snake held by the serpent-bearer Ophiuchus — You traced Serpens among the stars.

name: Sextans
mythology: Sextans represents a sextant, the navigational instrument. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century to commemorate his lost astronomical instruments. It is a faint constellation lying between Leo and Hydra.
discovery_badge_caption: Sextans represents a sextant, the navigational instrument — You found Sextans.
mastery_badge_caption: Sextans represents a sextant, the navigational instrument — You traced Sextans among the stars.

name: Taurus
mythology: Taurus represents a bull. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the Cretan Bull, which Heracles captured as one of his labors. It also represents the form Zeus took when he abducted Europa. It contains the Hyades and Pleiades star clusters, and the bright star Aldebaran.
discovery_badge_caption: Taurus represents a bull — You found Taurus.
mastery_badge_caption: Taurus represents a bull — You traced Taurus among the stars.

name: Telescopium
mythology: Telescopium represents a telescope. It was introduced by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century to commemorate the astronomical instrument. It lies south of Sagittarius and Corona Australis and contains few bright stars.
discovery_badge_caption: Telescopium represents a telescope — You found Telescopium.
mastery_badge_caption: Telescopium represents a telescope — You traced Telescopium among the stars.

name: Triangulum
mythology: Triangulum represents a triangle. In Greek mythology, it is identified with the island of Sicily, which Demeter asked Zeus to place in the sky. It has been recognized since ancient times and was listed by Ptolemy. Its name derives from its three brightest stars forming a triangle.
discovery_badge_caption: Triangulum represents a triangle — You found Triangulum.
mastery_badge_caption: Triangulum represents a triangle — You traced Triangulum among the stars.

name: Triangulum Australe
mythology: Triangulum Australe represents a southern triangle. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It is a small but distinct triangle of bright stars in the southern sky, with Atria as its brightest star.
discovery_badge_caption: Triangulum Australe represents a southern triangle — You found Triangulum Australe.
mastery_badge_caption: Triangulum Australe represents a southern triangle — You traced Triangulum Australe among the stars.

name: Tucana
mythology: Tucana represents a toucan, the tropical bird. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century. It contains the Small Magellanic Cloud, a satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, and the brilliant globular cluster 47 Tucanae.
discovery_badge_caption: Tucana represents a toucan, the tropical bird — You found Tucana.
mastery_badge_caption: Tucana represents a toucan, the tropical bird — You traced Tucana among the stars.

name: Ursa Major
mythology: Ursa Major represents the great bear. In Greek mythology, it is associated with Callisto, a nymph transformed into a bear by the jealous Hera. Her son Arcas almost killed her while hunting, but Zeus placed them both in the sky as Ursa Major and Boötes. Its seven brightest stars form the Big Dipper.
discovery_badge_caption: Ursa Major represents the great bear — You found Ursa Major.
mastery_badge_caption: Ursa Major represents the great bear — You traced Ursa Major among the stars.

name: Ursa Minor
mythology: Ursa Minor represents the lesser bear. In Greek mythology, it was formed from the transformed Arcas or one of the bears of Callisto. It contains Polaris, the North Star, which lies very close to the north celestial pole and has been used for navigation for centuries.
discovery_badge_caption: Ursa Minor represents the lesser bear — You found Ursa Minor.
mastery_badge_caption: Ursa Minor represents the lesser bear — You traced Ursa Minor among the stars.

name: Vela
mythology: Vela represents the sails of a ship. It was originally part of Argo Navis, the ship of Jason and the Argonauts, and was split off by Nicolas Louis de Lacaille in the 18th century. It contains several bright stars including Gamma Velorum, a bright Wolf-Rayet star.
discovery_badge_caption: Vela represents the sails of a ship — You found Vela.
mastery_badge_caption: Vela represents the sails of a ship — You traced Vela among the stars.

name: Virgo
mythology: Virgo represents a maiden or virgin. In Greek mythology, she is identified with several goddesses including Astraea, the goddess of justice, and Demeter, the goddess of the harvest. She holds the scales of justice. It is the second-largest constellation and its brightest star is Spica.
discovery_badge_caption: Virgo represents a maiden or virgin — You found Virgo.
mastery_badge_caption: Virgo represents a maiden or virgin — You traced Virgo among the stars.

name: Volans
mythology: Volans represents a flying fish. It was introduced by Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman in the late 16th century as Piscis Volans. It is a faint southern constellation with no bright stars, lying between Carina and Mensa.
discovery_badge_caption: Volans represents a flying fish — You found Volans.
mastery_badge_caption: Volans represents a flying fish — You traced Volans among the stars.

name: Vulpecula
mythology: Vulpecula represents a little fox. It was introduced by Johannes Hevelius in the 17th century as Vulpecula cum Ansere, the little fox with the goose. The goose was later dropped from the name. It contains the Dumbbell Nebula, the first planetary nebula discovered.
discovery_badge_caption: Vulpecula represents a little fox — You found Vulpecula.
mastery_badge_caption: Vulpecula represents a little fox — You traced Vulpecula among the stars.
